---
title: "Boundary Impact Model"
subtitle: "Modeling authorization boundaries and their governance implications"
doc-type: architecture-explainer
audience: [Customer, Technical, Architect]
classification: Controlled
boundary: GCC-Moderate
canon-source: "docs/docs/gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd"
canon-reference:
  - "docs/docs/gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd"
  - "docs/docs/drift-detection-boundary-amendment.qmd"
  - "docs/customer-documents/compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd"
derived-from: "uiao canon"
---

# Boundary Impact Model

The **boundary impact model** is UIAO's architectural treatment of what
happens when authorization boundaries cut across the substrate — most
notably the GCC-Moderate boundary that constrains telemetry, identity, and
enforcement surfaces inside Microsoft 365 GCC-Moderate tenants.

::: {.callout-note}
## Canon-anchor note
The original ROADMAP cited `docs/docs/authorization-boundary.qmd`, which
does not exist. This page anchors instead on the existing GCC-Moderate
boundary canon (`gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd`,
`gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd`,
`drift-detection-boundary-amendment.qmd`) and the customer-facing leaf
[`B.1 GCC-Moderate Boundary Model`](../compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd).
A canon-change ADR may later promote a generalized authorization-boundary
canon document; until then, this model is GCC-Moderate-anchored.
:::

[IMAGE-01: Boundary impact four-dimension diagram. A vertical boundary line down the center labeled "Authorization Boundary" splits the page into left ("Outside" — commercial / non-boundary side) and right ("Inside" — GCC-Moderate side). Four horizontal lanes cross the boundary, each labeled with one impact dimension: Telemetry Fidelity, Identity Authority, Enforcement Responsibility, Provenance Classification. On each lane the boundary line shows a transformation badge (e.g. "authoritative → derived"). Below each lane, a small drift-class badge indicates the drift class that fires on violation (DRIFT-SCHEMA, DRIFT-IDENTITY, DRIFT-AUTHZ, DRIFT-SEMANTIC). Clean engineering blueprint style, dark navy (#0D1B2E) and teal (#1E8C8C) on white background. No photographs, purely diagrammatic.]

## What the model captures

A boundary in UIAO is **not just a network or tenant edge** — it is any
surface across which:

- **Telemetry fidelity changes** (e.g. commercial → GCC-Moderate causes
  silent loss of Endpoint Analytics, Device Health Attestation, etc.).
- **Identity authority changes** (e.g. a federated trust crosses from one
  IdP's namespace to another).
- **Enforcement responsibility changes** (e.g. a control that was platform-
  enforced becomes agency-enforced, or vice versa).
- **Provenance classification changes** (e.g. a claim that was
  `authoritative` from system X becomes `derived` after crossing the
  boundary).

Each of these axes contributes an **impact dimension** that the model
quantifies independently.

## The four impact dimensions

| Dimension | Question | Drift class on violation |
|---|---|---|
| **Telemetry fidelity** | What signals are *actually* available on this side of the boundary? | `DRIFT-SCHEMA` (missing fields), `DRIFT-PROVENANCE` (broken chain) |
| **Identity authority** | Who can the issuer of a claim be resolved to here? | `DRIFT-IDENTITY` |
| **Enforcement responsibility** | Who must enforce this control on this side? | `DRIFT-AUTHZ` (out-of-envelope action) |
| **Provenance classification** | Is the claim still authoritative on this side, or only derived? | `DRIFT-SEMANTIC` |

The model deliberately maps onto the
[Drift Engine](drift-engine.qmd)'s five-class taxonomy. Boundary crossings
are the most common source of multi-class drift, and treating boundary
modeling as a drift problem (rather than a separate compliance concern)
keeps both surfaces in one operational language.

## The invisible-boundary problem (worked example)

The structural example for this model is the **GCC-Moderate invisible
boundary**, documented in
[`gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd`](../../docs/gcc-boundary-problem-statement.qmd):

> Microsoft 365 GCC-Moderate provides the same nominal product set as M365
> commercial, but the FedRAMP Moderate authorization boundary materially
> constrains the outbound telemetry that drives many of the platform's
> higher-order analytics. Features appear available in administrative
> portals, are described in commercial documentation, and may be referenced
> in SSPs and migration plans, but silently produce no data, emit no
> errors, and have no documented resolution path in the GCC-Moderate
> context.

Mapped onto the four-dimension model:

- **Telemetry fidelity:** Endpoint Analytics, Device Health Attestation,
  Locations / Network Fence, ARC Server Monitoring, and Expedited Updates
  are unavailable in GCC-Moderate despite UI presence. The boundary
  *silently* attenuates telemetry.
- **Identity authority:** federated trusts from commercial IdPs cross into
  GCC-Moderate scope; resolution rules differ on each side.
- **Enforcement responsibility:** controls that commercial customers
  consume as platform features (e.g. some ZT analytics) become **agency
  responsibility** in GCC-Moderate.
- **Provenance classification:** an `authoritative` claim sourced from a
  commercial-only telemetry path is `derived` (or absent) in GCC-Moderate.

Treating these as a single boundary impact rather than four unrelated
issues is what allows the
[B.1 GCC-Moderate Boundary Model](../compliance/boundary-authorization/B1-gcc-moderate-boundary-model.qmd)
leaf to produce a single coherent SSP narrative instead of four disjoint
ones.

[IMAGE-02: GCC-Moderate invisible-boundary visualization. Top half shows the M365 GCC-Moderate admin portal with five tiles labeled Endpoint Analytics / Device Health Attestation / Locations & Network Fence / ARC Server Monitoring / Expedited Updates — all displayed as available (green check). Bottom half shows what actually functions inside the boundary — the same five tiles redrawn empty or grayed out (red X), with explanatory captions like "telemetry blocked", "planning phase", "explicitly unavailable". Between the two halves, a horizontal "Invisible Boundary" line indicates where appearance and reality diverge. Right margin shows four UIAO compensating mechanisms (Boundary Probe, In-Boundary Telemetry, Compensating Enforcement, ATO Documentation Surface) as small numbered boxes. Clean engineering diagram, dark navy (#0D1B2E) and teal (#1E8C8C) primary, with red (#C74040) reserved for the unavailable-state tiles. No photographs, purely diagrammatic.]

## How UIAO compensates: in-boundary equivalents

Per
[`gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd`](../../docs/gcc-boundary-solution-architecture.qmd),
UIAO addresses each impact dimension through in-boundary mechanisms rather
than waiting for Microsoft to fix the platform:

1. **Boundary probe** — programmatically tests which features actually
   function on each side, producing a structured capability disposition
   instead of a portal screenshot.
2. **In-boundary telemetry** — adapters that operate inside the
   GCC-Moderate boundary and emit canonical claims with explicit
   `source_classification: derived` when they substitute for an
   unavailable commercial signal.
3. **Compensating enforcement** — agency-side enforcement adapters for
   controls that platform features cannot provide, with their evidence
   flowing through the same [Evidence Chain](evidence-chain.qmd).
4. **ATO documentation surface** — the boundary impact is captured as a
   first-class artifact that the SSP, POA&M, and Component Definition all
   reference, rather than as a series of footnotes.

## Why this is an architectural concern, not a compliance concern

Two reasons the boundary impact model lives in the Architecture Series and
not in the Compliance section:

1. **It changes the topology of the substrate.** A boundary crossing
   redefines which plane is authoritative for which claim. That is a
   structural property of the architecture, not a control mapping.
2. **It is the same problem on every boundary.** GCC-Moderate is the
   worked example, but identity-federation boundaries, classified-network
   boundaries, and agency-to-agency boundaries all instantiate the same
   four-dimension model. Solving it once architecturally is cheaper than
   re-solving it per compliance regime.

## What this means for an ATO package

A UIAO-anchored ATO package treats every authorization boundary as a
**named, modeled, drift-monitored surface**:

- The boundary itself appears in the SSP, with disposition data per the
  four impact dimensions.
- Compensating mechanisms are documented per impact dimension, not as
  generic mitigations.
- Drift findings cite the boundary they crossed (e.g.
  `DRIFT-PROVENANCE @ gcc-moderate-edge`), making remediation scope
  explicit.

This is what makes the GCC-Moderate boundary tractable: the substrate
treats the boundary itself as a first-class governed object, not as an
implicit assumption embedded in every control narrative.
